subtopic_detail: Necrotising fasciitis recognition and urgent management
stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and obesity presents with a 3-day history of rapidly spreading redness, swelling, and severe pain in his left lower leg following a minor skin abrasion. He has been on oral flucloxacillin from his GP for 48 hours without improvement. On examination, the erythema has extended 8 cm beyond the pen markings placed yesterday. The skin is dusky with two haemorrhagic bullae. He describes the pain as out of proportion to the visible changes. Crepitus is palpable over the medial calf.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 C, HR 122 bpm, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 96%.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24.8 x10^9/L, CRP 342 mg/L, Na 131 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 88), glucose 18.4 mmol/L, Hb 108 g/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L. X-ray left leg: subcutaneous gas.

lead_in: What is the most important next step in management?

options.text: Switch oral flucloxacillin to IV flucloxacillin 2 g every 6 hours and observe
options.rationale: Escalating antibiotics alone without surgical intervention is inadequate for necrotising fasciitis. While IV antibiotics are necessary, the defining treatment is urgent surgical debridement. Necrotising fasciitis cannot be cured with antibiotics alone due to thrombosis of nutrient vessels causing tissue necrosis beyond antibiotic reach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; eTG)

options.text: Urgent surgical debridement with IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and resuscitation
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. The key clinical features here are pain out of proportion to visible changes, rapid spread despite antibiotics, haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus, subcutaneous gas on X-ray, and sepsis. Urgent surgical debridement is the single most important intervention and should not be delayed for imaging. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. meropenem plus clindamycin plus vancomycin) and aggressive fluid resuscitation should be commenced simultaneously. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; ANZCA/RACS Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection Guidelines)
source_refs: ANZCA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection Guidelines

options.text: Urgent MRI of the left leg to confirm fascial involvement before any surgery
options.rationale: While MRI can identify fascial oedema and fluid tracking, imaging should not delay surgical exploration when the clinical diagnosis is strongly suspected. This patient has crepitus, subcutaneous gas on X-ray, and clinical sepsis. Surgical exploration is both diagnostic and therapeutic. MRI would cause dangerous delay. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Incision and drainage at the bedside as this is likely a deep soft tissue abscess
options.rationale: Bedside incision and drainage is appropriate for a localised abscess but is inadequate for necrotising fasciitis, which requires extensive surgical debridement often involving large areas of tissue excision. The clinical features (pain disproportionate to appearance, crepitus, gas on X-ray, rapid spread, bullae) distinguish necrotising fasciitis from an abscess. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the primary treatment with antibiotics as adjunct
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen has been used as an adjunct in some centres for gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis) but is not a substitute for surgical debridement. It should never delay surgery. Evidence for hyperbaric oxygen in necrotising fasciitis is limited and it is not standard first-line treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a rapidly progressive soft tissue infection involving the fascia and subcutaneous tissues with high mortality (20-40%). Key clinical features include pain out of proportion to visible changes, rapid progression despite antibiotics, haemorrhagic bullae, skin necrosis, crepitus, and systemic sepsis. Risk factors include diabetes, obesity, and immunosuppression. The definitive treatment is urgent surgical debridement combined with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics. Delay in surgery is the primary determinant of mortality.
key_points: Pain out of proportion to visible changes is the earliest and most important clinical sign
Subcutaneous gas and crepitus indicate gas-forming organisms but are not always present
Surgical debridement is definitive - antibiotics alone are insufficient
Do not delay surgery for imaging when the clinical diagnosis is clear
explanation.pearls: The LRINEC (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotising Fasciitis) score uses CRP > 150 mg/L, WCC > 15 x10^9/L, Hb < 135 g/L, Na < 135 mmol/L, creatinine > 141 micromol/L, and glucose > 10 mmol/L; a score of 6 or more has approximately 92% positive predictive value, but clinical suspicion should override a low score.

sources.label: ANZCA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection Guidelines

stem: A 28-year-old woman undergoing IVF presents 5 days after oocyte retrieval with abdominal distension, nausea, and reduced urine output over 24 hours. She had 22 oocytes retrieved and received a trigger injection of hCG 10,000 IU. Fresh embryo transfer was performed 3 days ago. On examination she has tense ascites and bilateral pleural effusions. Her weight has increased by 4 kg since retrieval. Bloods show haemoconcentration and elevated creatinine.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104 bpm, BP 98/62 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 94% on room air, T 37.2 C.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 162 g/L (baseline 132), haematocrit 0.49, WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, creatinine 128 micromol/L (baseline 64), Na 128 mmol/L, K 5.4 mmol/L, albumin 22 g/L, urine output last 12 hours: 180 mL. US: bilateral enlarged ovaries (right 12 cm, left 10 cm), moderate ascites, bilateral pleural effusions.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: IV crystalloid resuscitation with albumin infusion and VTE thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: This is severe OHSS (haemoconcentration, oliguria, ascites, pleural effusions, hyponatraemia). Management is supportive: IV crystalloid for intravascular volume depletion (despite total body fluid excess), IV human albumin 25% to maintain oncotic pressure, VTE thromboprophylaxis with LMWH (OHSS carries a very high VTE risk due to haemoconcentration and immobility), and strict fluid balance monitoring. Paracentesis may be needed for tense ascites causing respiratory compromise. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 5)

options.text: Aggressive IV diuresis with furosemide 40 mg to treat the fluid overload state
options.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in OHSS. Although there is significant third-space fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusions), the intravascular compartment is depleted (haemoconcentration, tachycardia, hypotension). Diuretics would worsen intravascular depletion, haemoconcentration, and renal impairment, potentially precipitating thromboembolic events. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Guideline)

options.text: Urgent surgical drainage of bilateral ovarian cysts under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgical intervention on the ovaries is not appropriate management for OHSS. The enlarged ovaries are a result of multiple stimulated follicles/corpora lutea and will resolve spontaneously over 1-3 weeks. Surgery risks ovarian torsion, haemorrhage, and loss of the pregnancy. Supportive management is the standard approach. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Guideline)

options.text: Fluid restriction to 1 litre daily to prevent worsening ascites and effusions
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is inappropriate because the intravascular compartment is volume-depleted despite the total body excess. The pathophysiology of OHSS involves increased capillary permeability with massive fluid shifts to the third space. Restricting fluids worsens haemoconcentration and renal impairment. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 5)

options.text: Commence cabergoline 0.5 mg daily to reduce VEGF-mediated capillary permeability
options.rationale: Cabergoline (a dopamine agonist that reduces VEGF receptor-2 expression) has evidence for prevention of OHSS when given at the time of trigger, but its role in treating established severe OHSS is limited. In a patient with oliguria, haemoconcentration, and hypotension, supportive management with IV fluids, albumin, and thromboprophylaxis is the priority. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Guideline)

explanation.summary: Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome is an iatrogenic complication of ovarian stimulation, most commonly following hCG trigger in IVF. The pathophysiology involves VEGF-mediated increased capillary permeability causing massive third-space fluid shifts. Severe OHSS presents with haemoconcentration, oliguria, electrolyte disturbance, ascites, and pleural effusions. Management is supportive with IV crystalloid to maintain intravascular volume, IV albumin, thromboprophylaxis, and close monitoring. Diuretics are contraindicated. The condition is self-limiting but can last 2-3 weeks if pregnancy is established.
key_points: OHSS involves intravascular depletion despite total body fluid excess
Diuretics are contraindicated as they worsen intravascular depletion
LMWH thromboprophylaxis is essential due to very high VTE risk
Paracentesis may be needed for respiratory compromise from tense ascites
explanation.pearls: Late-onset OHSS (occurring 10-14 days after hCG trigger) is driven by endogenous hCG from an implanting pregnancy and tends to be more prolonged and severe than early-onset OHSS; the shift from hCG to GnRH agonist triggers and freeze-all embryo strategies has dramatically reduced severe OHSS rates.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P3 woman has just delivered a 4,200 g baby vaginally after an 18-hour labour augmented with oxytocin. Active management of the third stage was performed. Ten minutes after placental delivery, she develops heavy vaginal bleeding estimated at 800 mL and ongoing. Uterine examination reveals a boggy, poorly contracted uterus. The placenta has been examined and appears complete. Bimanual uterine massage has been commenced. An IV oxytocin infusion of 40 IU in 1 L normal saline is running. Her blood pressure is currently 108/66 mmHg with a heart rate of 112 bpm.

data_table.Investigations: EBL 800 mL and ongoing. Placenta complete on inspection. No genital tract laceration on examination.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step for this atonic uterus?

options.text: Increase the oxytocin infusion rate to 80 IU in 1 L and continue massage
options.rationale: Increasing oxytocin beyond 40 IU is generally not recommended as the myometrium has a finite number of oxytocin receptors, and receptor saturation occurs after prolonged labour with oxytocin augmentation. Higher doses increase the risk of water intoxication and hyponatraemia without additional uterotonic benefit. A second-line agent is required. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline; WHO PPH Recommendations)

options.text: Sublingual misoprostol 200 mcg as a single dose for uterotonic effect here
options.rationale: Misoprostol 200 mcg is insufficient for treating established PPH. The recommended dose for PPH treatment is 800-1000 mcg rectally or sublingually. Furthermore, when an atonic uterus has not responded to first-line oxytocin, more potent uterotonics such as ergometrine or carboprost should be considered before misoprostol. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the next pharmacological step in PPH management
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is recommended as an adjunct in PPH management (WOMAN trial) but is an antifibrinolytic, not a uterotonic. It does not address the underlying uterine atony. A second-line uterotonic agent is needed to achieve uterine contraction. Tranexamic acid should be given concurrently but does not replace uterotonic escalation. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline; WOMAN Trial)

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g plus metronidazole 500 mg for suspected endometritis component
options.rationale: Endometritis is not the likely cause of PPH immediately after delivery. The boggy uterus after prolonged augmented labour is classic uterine atony. Antibiotics do not address the haemorrhage. While antibiotics may be indicated later if intrauterine procedures are performed, the immediate priority is uterotonic escalation. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 mcg combined with ongoing bimanual massage and oxytocin
options.rationale: Ergometrine 250 mcg IM is an appropriate second-line uterotonic when oxytocin alone is insufficient for atonic PPH. It produces sustained uterine contraction through direct smooth muscle action. Alternatively, IM carboprost (250 mcg) can be used. These should be given alongside ongoing bimanual massage and the oxytocin infusion. Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension, but this patient is normotensive. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline; WHO PPH Recommendations)

explanation.summary: Postpartum haemorrhage from uterine atony is the most common cause of PPH, accounting for approximately 70-80% of cases. Management follows a stepwise approach: bimanual uterine massage, first-line IV oxytocin, then second-line uterotonics (ergometrine or carboprost), followed by intrauterine balloon tamponade if pharmacological measures fail. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given as an adjunct within 3 hours of onset. Risk factors for atonic PPH include prolonged augmented labour, macrosomia, grand multiparity, and polyhydramnios.
key_points: Oxytocin is first-line but receptor saturation limits efficacy after prolonged augmentation
Ergometrine 250 mcg IM is a second-line uterotonic (contraindicated in hypertension)
Carboprost 250 mcg IM is an alternative (contraindicated in asthma)
Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic adjunct, not a uterotonic replacement
explanation.pearls: Ergometrine has an onset of action of 2-5 minutes IM (faster IV but higher risk of hypertension and nausea) and produces sustained tetanic uterine contraction lasting 60-90 minutes, compared to the rhythmic contractions produced by oxytocin which last only 15-30 minutes per bolus.

subtopic_detail: Rescue cerclage for cervical insufficiency with membrane prolapse
stem: A 26-year-old G3P0 woman at 20 weeks and 3 days gestation presents with a sensation of vaginal pressure but no contractions. She has a history of two mid-trimester losses: one at 19 weeks and one at 21 weeks, both presenting with painless cervical dilatation. No cerclage was placed in this pregnancy due to late booking. On speculum examination, the cervix is 3 cm dilated with bulging amniotic membranes visible at the external os. There is no vaginal bleeding, no evidence of membrane rupture, and no signs of chorioamnionitis. The membranes appear intact. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a cervical length of 0 mm with funnelling to the external os. CTG shows no uterine activity. Vaginal swabs for infection are sent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 88 bpm, BP 116/72 mmHg, WCC 9.8 x10^9/L, CRP 4 mg/L.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: cervical length 0 mm, funnelling to external os, membranes bulging through os. Foetal biometry consistent with 20 weeks. AFI 14 cm. No uterine activity on tocography.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management after counselling about risks and benefits?

options.text: Expectant management with bed rest and serial cervical length measurements only
options.rationale: With a fully dilated cervix, bulging membranes, and a history of two mid-trimester losses from cervical insufficiency, expectant management alone carries a very high risk of imminent delivery at a previable or periviable gestation. Without cerclage, the median latency in this situation is approximately 1-2 weeks, with delivery often before viability. (Source: RCOG Cervical Cerclage Guideline; Cochrane Review Cerclage)

options.text: Termination of pregnancy counselling as rescue cerclage has unacceptable risks
options.rationale: While rescue cerclage carries risks (membrane rupture, chorioamnionitis, preterm labour), outcomes without intervention are uniformly poor at this gestation. Rescue cerclage prolongs pregnancy by a median of 5-8 weeks with neonatal survival rates of 60-80% in selected patients. The decision should be made after careful counselling, not assumed to be futile. (Source: RCOG Cervical Cerclage Guideline)

options.text: Emergency rescue cerclage after amniocentesis to exclude infection and tocolysis
options.rationale: Emergency rescue (salvage) cerclage is appropriate in this scenario: cervical insufficiency with dilated cervix and bulging membranes, no chorioamnionitis (normal temperature, WCC, CRP), intact membranes, and a foetus at a gestation where prolongation would significantly improve outcomes. Pre-operative amniocentesis to exclude subclinical chorioamnionitis (elevated IL-6, positive culture) improves patient selection. Pre-operative tocolysis (indomethacin or nifedipine) and Trendelenburg positioning facilitate membrane reduction. The procedure extends median latency by 5-8 weeks. Perioperative antibiotics and progesterone supplementation are typically given. (Source: RCOG Cervical Cerclage Guideline; Cochrane Review Cerclage; AJOG Rescue Cerclage Meta-analysis)

options.text: Cervical pessary insertion as a less invasive alternative to surgical cerclage
options.rationale: Cervical pessaries have been studied for cervical shortening but have not been validated for the scenario of fully dilated cervix with bulging membranes. The PECEP trial and subsequent studies showed mixed results for pessaries in short cervix; they are not a substitute for rescue cerclage when the cervix is already open with membrane prolapse. (Source: RCOG Cervical Cerclage Guideline)

options.text: Intramuscular progesterone 250 mg weekly alone as cervical insufficiency treatment
options.rationale: Progesterone supplementation may be used as an adjunct to cerclage but is insufficient as sole management for an actively dilating cervix with bulging membranes. Progesterone's role is in prevention of recurrent preterm birth in women with short cervix, not in managing acute cervical insufficiency with membrane prolapse. (Source: RCOG Cervical Cerclage Guideline)

explanation.summary: Rescue (emergency or salvage) cerclage is a procedure performed when the cervix has already dilated and membranes are prolapsing through the os. Patient selection is critical: absence of chorioamnionitis, intact membranes, no active bleeding, and gestational age typically between 16 and 24 weeks. Pre-operative amniocentesis to exclude subclinical infection improves outcomes. The procedure involves Trendelenburg positioning, bladder filling to push membranes up, and placement of a cervical suture. Outcomes are significantly better than expectant management, with median prolongation of pregnancy by 5-8 weeks and neonatal survival of 60-80%.
key_points: Rescue cerclage is appropriate for cervical insufficiency with dilated cervix and intact membranes
Pre-operative amniocentesis excludes subclinical chorioamnionitis
Normal maternal inflammatory markers are essential before proceeding
Median pregnancy prolongation is 5-8 weeks with neonatal survival 60-80%
explanation.pearls: The McDonald cerclage technique using a non-absorbable braided suture placed at the cervicovaginal junction is the standard approach for rescue cerclage; the Shirodkar technique (placing the suture higher at the internal os) is technically more difficult with bulging membranes and is typically reserved for elective cerclage.

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy versus HELLP syndrome
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with 5 days of nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, and progressive malaise. She has no history of hypertension or liver disease. On examination she appears mildly jaundiced. Her blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg. There is no peripheral oedema. Blood results show markedly elevated transaminases, hypoglycaemia, hypofibrinogenaemia, and elevated bilirubin. Platelets are mildly reduced. Urine PCR is 12 mg/mmol. Her blood film shows no schistocytes or haemolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, T 37.4 C.
data_table.Investigations: ALT 412 U/L, AST 388 U/L, bilirubin 82 micromol/L (conjugated 68), ALP 320 U/L, albumin 24 g/L, glucose 2.8 mmol/L, ammonia 68 micromol/L (elevated), platelets 98 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 1.2 g/L (low), PT 18 s (elevated), APTT 42 s (elevated), LDH 310 U/L, haptoglobin 0.8 g/L (normal), blood film: no schistocytes. Urine PCR 12 mg/mmol. Uric acid 0.48 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: HELLP syndrome based on the liver enzyme elevation and low platelet count
options.rationale: HELLP syndrome requires haemolysis (elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, schistocytes), elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets. This patient has no evidence of haemolysis: haptoglobin is normal, blood film shows no schistocytes, and bilirubin elevation is predominantly conjugated (hepatocellular, not haemolytic). The coagulopathy and hypoglycaemia are also more characteristic of AFLP. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; Knight et al. UKOSS AFLP study)

options.text: Viral hepatitis based on the jaundice and markedly elevated liver transaminases
options.rationale: Viral hepatitis can cause elevated transaminases and jaundice in pregnancy, but the coagulopathy with hypofibrinogenaemia, hypoglycaemia, and elevated ammonia in the third trimester strongly suggest hepatic synthetic failure from AFLP rather than viral hepatitis. Viral serology should be sent but AFLP must be treated emergently. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines)

options.text: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy with unusually severe liver involvement
options.rationale: ICP causes pruritus as the predominant symptom with mild transaminase elevation (typically ALT < 300 U/L) and elevated bile acids. This patient has marked transaminase elevation, jaundice, coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia, and no pruritus, which are inconsistent with ICP and point to AFLP. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 43)

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy given the coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia and jaundice
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic for AFLP: third-trimester onset with nausea, vomiting, RUQ pain, jaundice, hypoglycaemia (from impaired hepatic gluconeogenesis), coagulopathy with hypofibrinogenaemia (from impaired hepatic synthesis), elevated ammonia, and conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. The absence of haemolysis (normal haptoglobin, no schistocytes) and absence of hypertension distinguish AFLP from HELLP. The Swansea criteria for AFLP are met. Urgent delivery is the definitive treatment. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; Ch'ng et al. Swansea criteria; Knight et al. UKOSS)

options.text: Pre-eclampsia with severe features given the liver and coagulation abnormalities
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia with severe features can cause liver dysfunction and coagulopathy, but this patient has a normal blood pressure (128/78 mmHg), minimal proteinuria (PCR 12 mg/mmol), and the degree of hepatic synthetic failure (hypoglycaemia, hypofibrinogenaemia, hyperammonaemia) exceeds what is typically seen in pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening condition occurring in the third trimester, caused by microvesicular fatty infiltration of hepatocytes leading to hepatic failure. Key differentiating features from HELLP syndrome include hypoglycaemia, hypofibrinogenaemia, elevated ammonia, conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, absence of haemolysis, and often normal or only mildly elevated blood pressure. The Swansea criteria assist diagnosis. Management is urgent delivery, which is curative. Supportive care includes glucose infusion, correction of coagulopathy, and monitoring for complications including hepatic encephalopathy and renal failure.
key_points: AFLP features: hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, hyperammonaemia, conjugated jaundice
HELLP features: haemolysis (schistocytes, low haptoglobin), hypertension, unconjugated bilirubin
Swansea criteria: 6 or more of 15 criteria in the absence of another explanation
Urgent delivery is the definitive treatment; liver function usually recovers within 1-4 weeks
explanation.pearls: AFLP is associated with foetal long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (LCHAD) deficiency, an autosomal recessive fatty acid oxidation disorder; neonatal screening for LCHAD deficiency should be performed after delivery of a pregnancy complicated by AFLP.

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with gestational diabetes (insulin-requiring) is delivering vaginally at 39 weeks. After delivery of the foetal head, the head retracts tightly against the perineum (turtle sign) and the anterior shoulder does not deliver with the next contraction despite gentle downward traction. Shoulder dystocia is declared. The midwife calls for help. The time is noted. The mother is positioned with her buttocks at the edge of the bed. One minute has elapsed since delivery of the head.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first manoeuvre after calling for help?

options.text: McRoberts manoeuvre with simultaneous suprapubic pressure applied correctly
options.rationale: McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflexion of maternal hips onto the abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure is the recommended first-line manoeuvre for shoulder dystocia. It straightens the sacral promontory, increases the AP diameter of the pelvis, and rotates the pubic symphysis superiorly. Suprapubic pressure (applied posteriorly and laterally to the anterior foetal shoulder) helps adduct and rotate the shoulder under the symphysis. This combination resolves approximately 50-60% of shoulder dystocia cases. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 42)

options.text: Immediate delivery of the posterior arm by sweeping it across the foetal chest
options.rationale: Delivery of the posterior arm is an effective manoeuvre but is a second-line internal manoeuvre, not the first step. McRoberts with suprapubic pressure should be attempted first as it is less invasive, quicker to perform, and resolves the majority of cases without requiring internal manoeuvres. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Fundal pressure to push the foetus through the pelvis with maternal effort
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia. It pushes the anterior shoulder further against the pubic symphysis, worsening the impaction and increasing the risk of brachial plexus injury, foetal fractures, and uterine rupture. Suprapubic (not fundal) pressure is the correct technique. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 42)

options.text: Zavanelli manoeuvre to replace the foetal head and deliver by emergency caesarean
options.rationale: The Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement followed by emergency caesarean) is a last-resort procedure used only when all other manoeuvres have failed. It carries significant risks including foetal injury and is technically very difficult. It should not be attempted as the first intervention. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 42)

options.text: Episiotomy as the first intervention to create space for shoulder delivery
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not resolve shoulder dystocia because the obstruction is bony (anterior shoulder impacted behind the pubic symphysis), not a soft tissue problem. An episiotomy may be performed to facilitate internal manoeuvres if needed, but it is not the primary intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the anterior shoulder impacts behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the foetal head. The HELPERR mnemonic guides management: Help called, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs in McRoberts position, suprapubic Pressure, Enter for internal rotational manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll to all-fours. McRoberts with suprapubic pressure is the first-line manoeuvre. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated. Risk factors include macrosomia, maternal diabetes, and prior shoulder dystocia, but over 50% of cases occur unpredictably.
key_points: McRoberts with suprapubic pressure is first-line and resolves 50-60% of cases
Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated - it worsens impaction
Head-to-body delivery interval should ideally be less than 5 minutes
Episiotomy facilitates internal manoeuvres but does not resolve the bony obstruction
explanation.pearls: The Rubin II manoeuvre (applying pressure to the posterior aspect of the anterior shoulder to adduct and rotate it) and the Wood screw manoeuvre (rotating the posterior shoulder 180 degrees) are the standard internal rotational manoeuvres attempted after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail; they resolve an additional 20-30% of cases.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 Rh-negative woman at 16 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident in which she was a restrained front-seat passenger. She reports minor abdominal discomfort but no vaginal bleeding. She has no abdominal tenderness, and the uterus is non-tender and appropriate for dates. Foetal heart tones are heard on Doppler. Ultrasound confirms a viable intrauterine pregnancy with no retroplacental haematoma. Her blood group is O Rh-negative with no irregular antibodies on her most recent screen at 12 weeks. She has not yet received routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, T 36.7 C.
data_table.Investigations: Foetal Doppler: FHR 156 bpm. US: viable IUP, no retroplacental collection. Blood group: O Rh-negative. Antibody screen: negative. Kleihauer-Betke test: pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding Rh isoimmunisation prevention?

options.text: No anti-D required as there is no vaginal bleeding and the ultrasound is normal
options.rationale: Abdominal trauma in pregnancy is a sensitising event for Rh isoimmunisation regardless of whether vaginal bleeding is present. Fetomaternal haemorrhage can occur with abdominal trauma even without visible bleeding or ultrasound abnormalities. Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the event. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines; RANZCOG Anti-D in Pregnancy)

options.text: Anti-D 625 IU IM within 72 hours and Kleihauer to check for large fetomaternal bleed
options.rationale: At 16 weeks (beyond 12 weeks), the standard anti-D dose for a sensitising event is 625 IU IM, which covers a fetomaternal haemorrhage of up to 6 mL of foetal red cells. A Kleihauer-Betke test is performed to quantify the fetomaternal haemorrhage; if greater than 6 mL foetal red cells is detected, additional anti-D is required. Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the sensitising event. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines; RANZCOG Anti-D in Pregnancy)

options.text: Anti-D 250 IU IM as the standard dose regardless of gestational age at any event
options.rationale: Anti-D 250 IU is the dose for sensitising events before 12 weeks gestation. After 12 weeks, the larger foetal blood volume means a 625 IU dose is required to cover a standard fetomaternal haemorrhage. Using the smaller dose beyond 12 weeks risks inadequate prophylaxis. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Defer anti-D until the routine 28-week antenatal prophylaxis dose is administered
options.rationale: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis at 28 weeks is a separate indication from event-based anti-D. A sensitising event (abdominal trauma) requires anti-D within 72 hours regardless of when routine prophylaxis is due. Waiting until 28 weeks would allow sensitisation to occur. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Cross-match and transfuse Rh-negative packed red cells as a precautionary measure
options.rationale: Red cell transfusion is indicated for maternal anaemia or haemorrhagic shock, neither of which is present here. The patient is haemodynamically stable with no bleeding. The concern is fetomaternal haemorrhage and Rh sensitisation, which is addressed by anti-D immunoglobulin, not by blood transfusion. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anti-D immunoglobulin prevents Rh isoimmunisation in Rh-negative women exposed to Rh-positive foetal red cells. Sensitising events include abdominal trauma, antepartum haemorrhage, amniocentesis, external cephalic version, and miscarriage. The dose depends on gestational age: 250 IU before 12 weeks, 625 IU at or after 12 weeks. A Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage to determine whether additional anti-D is needed. Anti-D must be administered within 72 hours of the sensitising event.
key_points: Abdominal trauma is a sensitising event requiring anti-D regardless of bleeding
Dose: 250 IU before 12 weeks, 625 IU at or after 12 weeks
Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies FMH to determine need for additional anti-D
Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the sensitising event
explanation.pearls: One vial of anti-D 625 IU covers approximately 6 mL of foetal red cells (or 12 mL of whole foetal blood); for every additional 1 mL of foetal red cells detected on Kleihauer beyond 6 mL, an extra 100 IU of anti-D should be administered, with a repeat Kleihauer at 48 hours to confirm clearance.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy is referred at 20 weeks gestation after her routine ultrasound shows significant growth discordance and amniotic fluid discrepancy between the twins. Detailed ultrasound confirms one twin (donor) has a deepest vertical pool of 1.2 cm with a stuck appearance against the uterine wall, while the other (recipient) has polyhydramnios with a deepest vertical pool of 12 cm. The donor twin has an absent bladder on repeated imaging. Doppler studies show abnormal umbilical artery waveforms in the donor but normal in the recipient. Both twins have cardiac activity.

data_table.Investigations: Donor twin: DVP 1.2 cm (oligohydramnios), bladder not visualised, UA Doppler: absent end-diastolic flow. EFW 10th centile. Recipient twin: DVP 12 cm (polyhydramnios), distended bladder, UA Doppler: normal. EFW 50th centile. Quintero Stage III TTTS.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management for this stage of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome?

options.text: Serial amnioreduction every 2 to 3 days to decompress the polyhydramnios sac
options.rationale: Serial amnioreduction was the historical treatment for TTTS but only addresses the symptom (polyhydramnios) without treating the underlying cause (unbalanced placental vascular anastomoses). It has inferior survival rates compared to fetoscopic laser ablation, particularly in Quintero Stage III or higher. The Eurofetus trial demonstrated superior outcomes with laser. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline; Eurofetus Trial)

options.text: Expectant management with fortnightly ultrasound surveillance and growth scans
options.rationale: Expectant management of Quintero Stage III TTTS carries an extremely poor prognosis with dual foetal death rates exceeding 70-80% without intervention. The absent end-diastolic flow in the donor twin indicates significant circulatory compromise. Active intervention is required. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by cord occlusion to save the recipient
options.rationale: Selective reduction (cord occlusion of one twin) is a last resort when laser ablation has failed or is not feasible, or when one twin has severe anomalies. At Quintero Stage III with both twins alive, fetoscopic laser ablation offers the best chance of dual survival (approximately 60-70%) and should be attempted first. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Elective preterm delivery at 26 weeks after a course of antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Delivery at 26 weeks carries extreme prematurity risks and does not address the TTTS. At 20 weeks, the foetuses are previable for delivery. The goal is to treat the vascular imbalance and prolong the pregnancy as long as possible. Fetoscopic laser ablation achieves this most effectively. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses at a referral centre
options.rationale: Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of the communicating placental vascular anastomoses is the definitive treatment for Quintero Stage II-IV TTTS. It ablates the shared vessels causing the imbalanced transfusion and effectively creates two independent placental circulations. The Eurofetus randomised trial demonstrated superior survival of at least one twin (76% vs 56%) and reduced neurological morbidity compared to serial amnioreduction. This should be performed at a tertiary foetal medicine centre with expertise. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline; Eurofetus Trial; NICE Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome occurs in approximately 10-15% of monochorionic twin pregnancies due to unbalanced blood flow through shared placental vascular anastomoses. The Quintero staging system grades severity from Stage I (fluid discrepancy alone) to Stage V (foetal death). Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation is the standard treatment for Stage II and above, with overall survival of at least one twin approximately 85-90% and dual survival approximately 60-70%. The Solomon technique (dichorionisation of the entire vascular equator) reduces recurrence compared to selective laser of visible anastomoses alone.
key_points: TTTS affects 10-15% of monochorionic twins via unbalanced placental anastomoses
Quintero staging: I (fluid discrepancy), II (absent bladder), III (abnormal Doppler), IV (hydrops), V (death)
Fetoscopic laser is definitive treatment for Stage II-IV
Solomon technique reduces recurrence by dichorionising the vascular equator
explanation.pearls: Post-laser TTTS surveillance should include twice-weekly Doppler assessment for 2 weeks, as approximately 5-10% of cases develop twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence (TAPS) after laser treatment, detected by MCA-PSV discordance (> 1.5 MoM in one twin and < 1.0 MoM in the other).

subtopic_detail: Pelvic inflammatory disease outpatient antibiotic regimen
stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to her GP with 5 days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, and a new malodorous vaginal discharge. She has a new sexual partner of 6 weeks and inconsistent condom use. She is not pregnant. On examination she has bilateral adnexal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness. Her temperature is 37.6 degrees C. Endocervical swabs have been collected for Chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT, and a high vaginal swab for microscopy and culture. She is able to tolerate oral medications, is not vomiting, and is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 C, HR 82 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg.
data_table.Investigations: Urine pregnancy test: negative. Pelvic US: no tubo-ovarian abscess, minimal free fluid in pouch of Douglas. Swab results pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric outpatient antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose alone covering the most likely STI pathogen
options.rationale: Azithromycin alone covers Chlamydia trachomatis but does not cover Neisseria gonorrhoeae or anaerobic organisms commonly involved in PID. PID is typically polymicrobial and requires broad-spectrum empiric therapy covering Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and anaerobes. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines; eTG)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg twice daily for 14 days as sole treatment
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate has some anaerobic and gram-negative cover but does not reliably cover Chlamydia trachomatis, which is the most common causative organism in PID in young women. Doxycycline or azithromycin is essential for chlamydial cover. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: IM ceftriaxone 500 mg stat plus oral doxycycline 100 mg BD and metronidazole BD
options.rationale: The recommended Australian outpatient PID regimen is IM ceftriaxone 500 mg as a single dose (covering Neisseria gonorrhoeae) plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days (covering Chlamydia trachomatis and other susceptible organisms) plus oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days (covering anaerobes including Bacteroides and BV-associated organisms). This provides comprehensive polymicrobial coverage. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg single dose plus oral azithromycin 1 g single dose
options.rationale: Single-dose regimens are insufficient for PID treatment, which requires a 14-day course to adequately treat the deep pelvic infection. Additionally, fluoroquinolone resistance in N. gonorrhoeae exceeds 30% in Australia, making ciprofloxacin unreliable for empiric gonorrhoea cover. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days alone without ceftriaxone cover
options.rationale: Doxycycline alone does not cover Neisseria gonorrhoeae, which must be treated empirically in PID even before swab results are available, as untreated gonococcal PID causes more severe tubal damage. The course duration should also be 14 days, not 7 days. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Pelvic inflammatory disease is typically polymicrobial, involving Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and endogenous vaginal flora including anaerobes. Outpatient management is appropriate for mild-to-moderate PID without tubo-ovarian abscess, systemic toxicity, or inability to tolerate oral medications. The standard Australian outpatient regimen covers all three pathogen groups. Partner notification and treatment is essential. Follow-up at 48-72 hours and 2 weeks is recommended. Inpatient admission is indicated for severe PID, tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, or failure of outpatient therapy.
key_points: PID requires empiric coverage of Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and anaerobes
IM ceftriaxone plus oral doxycycline plus oral metronidazole for 14 days is standard
Outpatient management is appropriate for mild-moderate PID without complications
Partner notification and treatment is mandatory
explanation.pearls: Delayed treatment of PID increases the risk of tubal factor infertility: each episode of PID is associated with approximately an 8% risk of tubal factor infertility after the first episode, rising to approximately 20% after two episodes and 40% after three episodes.

subtopic_detail: Emergency contraception selection by weight and timing
stem: A 30-year-old woman with a BMI of 32 kg per square metre presents to a sexual health clinic 96 hours after unprotected sexual intercourse during what she estimates was her fertile window. She had a positive ovulation predictor kit 2 days before the unprotected intercourse. She is otherwise healthy with regular 28-day cycles. She does not want an intrauterine device due to prior difficult insertion and a planned MRI in 2 weeks. She asks about oral emergency contraception options given the time elapsed and her weight.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate oral emergency contraception recommendation?

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel 1.5 mg as it remains equally effective at any time up to 5 days
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel EC has significantly reduced efficacy beyond 72 hours and is less effective in women with BMI above 25-26. At 96 hours post-intercourse in a woman with BMI 32, levonorgestrel has very low expected efficacy. It is licensed for use up to 72 hours, not 5 days. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Double-dose levonorgestrel 3 mg to overcome the reduced efficacy at higher BMI
options.rationale: While some pharmacokinetic data suggest higher doses may improve levonorgestrel levels in women with higher BMI, double-dosing is not an approved or evidence-based recommendation in Australian or international guidelines. At 96 hours, even a double dose is unlikely to provide meaningful efficacy. The correct approach is to use ulipristal acetate. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel 1.5 mg now plus a second dose of 1.5 mg in 12 hours apart
options.rationale: Split-dose levonorgestrel (two 0.75 mg doses 12 hours apart) was an older regimen that has been superseded by the single 1.5 mg dose. Neither dosing schedule is effective at 96 hours post-intercourse, and split dosing does not improve efficacy in women with higher BMI. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Oral ulipristal acetate 30 mg as it maintains efficacy to 120 hours and at higher BMI
options.rationale: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg is effective up to 120 hours (5 days) post-intercourse and maintains greater efficacy than levonorgestrel in women with higher BMI. As a selective progesterone receptor modulator, UPA can delay ovulation even after the LH surge has begun, unlike levonorgestrel. At 96 hours with BMI 32 and the copper IUD declined, UPA is the most appropriate oral option. Pregnancy should be excluded before administration. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline; AMH; Glasier et al. Lancet 2010)

options.text: No oral EC is effective at 96 hours so advise waiting and performing a pregnancy test
options.rationale: This is incorrect. Ulipristal acetate is licensed and effective up to 120 hours (5 days) post-intercourse. At 96 hours, UPA still provides meaningful pregnancy risk reduction. Advising the patient that nothing can be done would deny her an effective intervention. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline)

explanation.summary: Emergency contraception selection should consider time since intercourse, BMI, and patient preferences. Ulipristal acetate maintains efficacy up to 120 hours and has less reduction in efficacy with increasing BMI compared to levonorgestrel. The copper IUD remains the most effective option at any time point but may not be acceptable to all patients. Levonorgestrel is most effective within 24 hours and has reduced efficacy beyond 72 hours and in women with BMI above 26. UPA should not be used with hormonal contraception for 5 days after administration due to its progesterone receptor modulating effects.
key_points: UPA is effective up to 120 hours; LNG is effective up to 72 hours
UPA maintains better efficacy than LNG at higher BMI
UPA can delay ovulation even after the LH surge has begun
Hormonal contraception should not be started for 5 days after UPA
explanation.pearls: A meta-analysis of EC trials showed that levonorgestrel's pregnancy rate doubles in women with BMI above 26 kg/m2 compared to normal weight women, while ulipristal acetate showed no statistically significant reduction in efficacy up to BMI of 35 kg/m2, making UPA the preferred oral EC for women with elevated BMI.
